title: Basic Nursing Care — Medication Administration (Set 3)
subtitle: Rights of med admin, identification, routes, safety, refusals & basic calculations
scenario: Administering medications safely: the rights of medication administration, correct identification, routes, handling refusals and reactions, and basic dosage calculations.

steps.q: Which set BEST represents the classic 'rights' of medication administration?

opts.text: Right patient, right drug, right dose, right route, right time
opts.rat: The core rights are right patient, drug, dose, route, and time (with right documentation and right reason added); they prevent medication errors.

opts.text: Right color, right smell, right size, right shape, right price
opts.rat: These are not the rights of medication administration.

opts.text: Right room, right bed, right meal, right blanket, right TV
opts.rat: These are unrelated to safe medication administration.

opts.text: Right mood, right weather, right day of week
opts.rat: These are irrelevant to medication safety.

steps.q: Before giving a medication, the technician should verify the client's identity using:

opts.text: The room number only
opts.rat: Room/bed numbers change and are not reliable identifiers.

opts.text: Two identifiers, such as name and date of birth
opts.rat: Using two identifiers (e.g., name and date of birth) confirms the right patient and prevents errors.

opts.text: Whoever answers to the name called out
opts.rat: Responding to a name is unreliable, especially with confused clients.

opts.text: The client's meal tray
opts.rat: A meal tray is not a valid identifier.

steps.q: Before administering any medication, the technician must always check the client's:

opts.text: Favorite color
opts.rat: This is irrelevant to medication safety.

opts.text: Shoe size
opts.rat: This has nothing to do with medication administration.

opts.text: Allergies
opts.rat: Checking allergies before every medication prevents potentially life-threatening allergic reactions.

opts.text: Television preferences
opts.rat: This is unrelated to medication safety.

steps.q: When should the technician document that a medication was given?

opts.text: Before preparing it, to save time
opts.rat: Charting before giving risks documenting a dose that was never taken.

opts.text: At the end of the week
opts.rat: Delayed documentation is inaccurate and unsafe.

opts.text: Only if the client asks
opts.rat: Documentation is required regardless of client request.

opts.text: Immediately after the client takes it
opts.rat: Medications are documented right after administration—never before—to keep the record accurate and prevent double-dosing.

steps.q: A client refuses their prescribed medication. The technician should:

opts.text: Respect the refusal, then document it and notify the nurse
opts.rat: A client may refuse medication; the technician documents the refusal and reports it so the team can follow up.

opts.text: Force the medication into the client's mouth
opts.rat: Forcing medication violates the client's rights and is unsafe.

opts.text: Hide the medication in food without telling anyone
opts.rat: Covertly medicating a client is a rights violation.

opts.text: Give a double dose next time
opts.rat: Doubling a dose is dangerous and never appropriate for a missed dose.

steps.q: The technician observes a client hiding a pill in the side of the mouth ('cheeking'). The BEST action is to:

opts.text: Ignore it if the client seems calm
opts.rat: Cheeking risks a missed dose or later overdose and must be addressed.

opts.text: Check the mouth to confirm the pill was swallowed and report cheeking to the nurse
opts.rat: Checking the mouth ensures the dose was taken; reporting cheeking allows the team to prevent hoarding or overdose.

opts.text: Let the client save the pill for later
opts.rat: Allowing hoarding is unsafe and can lead to overdose.

opts.text: Give another pill immediately without checking
opts.rat: Giving more without verifying risks a double dose.

steps.q: A client has difficulty swallowing pills. The technician knows that which type of tablet should NOT be crushed?

opts.text: Any tablet, since crushing is always safe
opts.rat: Not all tablets are safe to crush; some must be given whole.

opts.text: Only chewable vitamins
opts.rat: Chewables are meant to be chewed; the concern is enteric-coated/extended-release forms.

opts.text: Enteric-coated or extended-release tablets
opts.rat: Crushing enteric-coated or extended/sustained-release tablets destroys their design and can cause overdose or irritation; check before crushing any medication.

opts.text: Plain, immediate-release scored tablets
opts.rat: These are generally safe to crush/split; the hazard is coated or extended-release forms.

steps.q: A sublingual medication should be administered by having the client:

opts.text: Swallow it whole with a full glass of water
opts.rat: Swallowing defeats sublingual absorption.

opts.text: Chew it thoroughly and spit it out
opts.rat: This is not how sublingual medication works.

opts.text: Crush it into their food
opts.rat: This is not the correct sublingual route.

opts.text: Place it under the tongue and let it dissolve
opts.rat: Sublingual medications are placed under the tongue to dissolve and absorb through the mucosa; they should not be swallowed.

steps.q: After preparing medications, the technician should NEVER:

opts.text: Leave the medication unattended at the bedside for the client to take later
opts.rat: Leaving medications unattended risks the wrong person taking them, missed or hoarded doses; the technician stays until the dose is taken.

opts.text: Stay with the client while they take the dose
opts.rat: Staying to confirm the dose is taken is correct practice.

opts.text: Verify the client's identity first
opts.rat: Verifying identity is required, not prohibited.

opts.text: Document after administration
opts.rat: Documenting after administration is correct.

steps.q: A PRN medication is one that is given:

opts.text: On a fixed schedule regardless of need
opts.rat: That describes scheduled, not PRN, medication.

opts.text: As needed, after assessing that the client meets the criteria for it
opts.rat: PRN ('as needed') medications are given based on assessed need within the ordered parameters, and the reason and response are documented.

opts.text: Only once in a lifetime
opts.rat: PRN refers to as-needed dosing, not a one-time-ever rule.

opts.text: Without any assessment or documentation
opts.rat: PRN doses require assessment and documentation of reason and effect.

steps.q: The technician should verify a medication against the medication administration record (MAR):

opts.text: Never; checking once from memory is enough
opts.rat: Relying on memory increases the risk of error.

opts.text: Only after the client has taken it
opts.rat: Verification must occur before administration.

opts.text: Three times before administering it
opts.rat: The standard safety practice is to check the medication against the MAR three times before giving it.

opts.text: Only when a supervisor is watching
opts.rat: Verification is always required, not only when observed.

steps.q: A physician orders 500 mg of a medication. The tablets on hand are 250 mg each. How many tablets should the technician give?

opts.text: 1 tablet
opts.rat: One 250 mg tablet provides only 250 mg, half the ordered dose.

opts.text: 4 tablets
opts.rat: Four tablets would be 1,000 mg, double the ordered dose.

opts.text: Half a tablet
opts.rat: Half a tablet is 125 mg, far below the ordered dose.

opts.text: 2 tablets
opts.rat: Using desired ÷ have: 500 mg ÷ 250 mg = 2 tablets.

steps.q: A medication is ordered as 125 mg. The scored tablets available are 250 mg each. The technician should give:

opts.text: One-half (½) tablet
opts.rat: 125 mg ÷ 250 mg = 0.5, so half of a scored (splittable) tablet provides the ordered dose.

opts.text: 2 tablets
opts.rat: Two tablets would be 500 mg, four times the ordered dose.

opts.text: 3 tablets
opts.rat: This greatly exceeds the ordered dose.

opts.text: 1 whole tablet
opts.rat: One whole 250 mg tablet is double the ordered 125 mg.

steps.q: An oral liquid is ordered as 250 mg. The bottle is labeled 125 mg per 5 mL. How many mL should the technician give?

opts.text: 2.5 mL
opts.rat: 2.5 mL would provide only about 62.5 mg.

opts.text: 10 mL
opts.rat: 250 mg ÷ 125 mg = 2, and 2 × 5 mL = 10 mL delivers the ordered 250 mg.

opts.text: 5 mL
opts.rat: 5 mL provides 125 mg, half the ordered dose.

opts.text: 20 mL
opts.rat: 20 mL would deliver 500 mg, double the ordered dose.

steps.q: The technician should question or clarify a medication order when:

opts.text: It is written clearly and matches the client's care
opts.rat: A clear, appropriate order does not require clarification.

opts.text: The client simply prefers a different color pill
opts.rat: Color preference is not a reason to change an order.

opts.text: The order is unclear, incomplete, or appears unsafe
opts.rat: An unclear, incomplete, or seemingly unsafe order must be clarified with the prescriber before administering—never guessed.

opts.text: Never; all orders are given exactly as written no matter what
opts.rat: Unsafe or unclear orders must be questioned to protect the client.

steps.q: The technician should administer only medications that they:

opts.text: Found already poured by an unknown coworker
opts.rat: Giving a dose prepared by someone else risks errors you cannot verify.

opts.text: Discovered loose on the counter
opts.rat: Unlabeled, loose medication cannot be verified and must not be given.

opts.text: Guessed at from an unlabeled cup
opts.rat: Unlabeled medication must never be administered.

opts.text: Personally prepared and can verify
opts.rat: A person should give only medications they prepared themselves, so they can verify the drug, dose, and integrity; never give a dose drawn up by someone else.

steps.q: Controlled substances (e.g., certain sedatives) require the technician to:

opts.text: Follow secure storage and accurate counting/documentation per policy
opts.rat: Controlled substances require locked storage, accurate counts (often verified by two staff), and precise documentation of every dose and any waste.

opts.text: Store them loose in an open drawer
opts.rat: Controlled substances must be secured, not left openly accessible.

opts.text: Skip documentation to save time
opts.rat: Documentation of controlled substances is mandatory.

opts.text: Give them to any client who asks
opts.rat: They are given only per order to the correct client, with strict accountability.

steps.q: Some medications have 'hold parameters' (e.g., hold a blood-pressure drug if systolic BP is below a set value). This means the technician should:

opts.text: Give the medication regardless of the vital signs
opts.rat: Ignoring hold parameters can seriously harm the client.

opts.text: Check the relevant vital sign before giving and hold/report if it is outside the parameter
opts.rat: Hold parameters require checking the specified vital sign first and withholding the dose (and notifying the nurse) if the value falls outside the safe range.

opts.text: Double the dose if the vital sign is abnormal
opts.rat: Doubling a dose is never an appropriate response to a hold parameter.

opts.text: Ignore the parameter entirely
opts.rat: Hold parameters exist for safety and must be followed.

steps.q: Shortly after receiving a medication, a client develops hives, swelling, and difficulty breathing. The technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Give another dose of the same medication
opts.rat: Re-administering the suspected allergen could be fatal.

opts.text: Wait an hour to see if it worsens
opts.rat: Delaying care during a possible anaphylactic reaction is dangerous.

opts.text: Recognize a possible allergic/anaphylactic reaction and get help immediately
opts.rat: Hives, swelling, and breathing difficulty suggest a severe allergic reaction; this is an emergency requiring immediate help and rapid escalation.

opts.text: Document it at the end of the shift and take no other action
opts.rat: This is an emergency needing immediate intervention, not delayed charting.

steps.q: Which is an appropriate site for an intramuscular (IM) injection?

opts.text: The center of the abdomen
opts.rat: The abdomen is used for subcutaneous injections (e.g., insulin/heparin), not IM.

opts.text: The palm of the hand
opts.rat: The palm is not an injection site.

opts.text: The bottom of the foot
opts.rat: The sole of the foot is not an IM injection site.

opts.text: The ventrogluteal or deltoid muscle
opts.rat: The ventrogluteal site (and the deltoid for small volumes) are appropriate, well-established IM injection sites.

reference: Aligned to the California BVNPT Psychiatric Technician exam content area: Basic Nursing Care. Written from standard psychiatric-technician and nursing references. Pending faculty review.
